content.text: An affordance is a design property that communicates to a user what an object can do — a button affords clicking, a door handle affords pulling. In AI interfaces, affordances answer the question users silently ask the moment they land on your product: "What can I do here, and what is this thing capable of?" Getting affordances wrong means users either underuse the AI (because they don't know it can help) or overtrust it (because they were led to believe it can do more than it can).

attrs.alt: Four types of AI affordances: input (placeholder text, example chips), capability (feature badges, skill chips), boundary (scope indicators, knowledge cutoff), and interaction (regenerate, edit, feedback). Anti-patterns vs good patterns comparison.

content.text: Input Affordances: Teaching Users How to Ask
The most common failure in AI product design is a blank text box with no guidance. The empty state should be as informative as the populated one. Three techniques work consistently:
Scoped placeholder text
. Instead of "Ask me anything", use "Ask me about your account, orders, or returns". Scoped placeholders set accurate expectations and reduce abandonment.
Starter chips

. Three to five clickable sample questions shown beneath the input box. Users click a chip to see a real AI response, building confidence before they type their own question. Pick chips that showcase the AI's most valuable capabilities.
Contextual follow-up suggestions

. After the AI responds, show 2–3 suggested follow-up queries relevant to what was just discussed. This patterns exists in most consumer AI products because it effectively prevents conversation dead-ends.
Capability Affordances: Showing What the AI Knows

If the AI has access to the user's data — their account, their files, their calendar — make this explicit. Users who don't know the AI has context will repeat information it already has, eroding trust in the product's intelligence.
Boundary Affordances: Setting Accurate Expectations

The AI cannot do everything users will try to ask. Surface these boundaries proactively rather than letting the AI apologize reactively. A "knowledge cutoff" label, a scope disclaimer, or a confidence indicator primes users to treat AI outputs appropriately.
Interaction Affordances: Teaching the Control Model

Many users do not know they can edit a previous message, regenerate a response, or tell the AI to try again with different constraints. These controls must be visible — not hidden in a tooltip — because users who don't see them conclude the AI is wrong and abandon.
Thinking and Loading Affordances

Users who submit a query and see nothing for 2–3 seconds will assume the interface is broken. A visible thinking state — animated dots, a loading message, a partial streaming response — maintains trust during generation latency.
Affordance Audit Checklist
Does the empty state tell users what to ask, not just "ask me anything"?
Are 3–5 starter chips visible before the user types their first message?
Does the header or info panel list the data the AI has access to?
Is there a thinking/loading state visible within 100ms of submission?
Are Regenerate, Copy, and feedback buttons visible on every AI response (not just on hover on desktop)?
When the AI cannot help, does it offer an alternative action (escalate to human, link to docs)?
Is there a knowledge cutoff or data-freshness label visible somewhere in the UI?
Can users stop a long-running generation?
Common Mistakes
Affordance design for AI interfaces has a specific set of failure patterns that differ from conventional UI design. The following are the most frequently observed pitfalls:
"Ask me anything" placeholder text.

This sets unlimited expectations and provides no scoping signal. Users don't know what the AI actually handles and may abandon when they hit a limitation they didn't know existed. Replace with scoped placeholders like "Ask me about your orders, returns, or shipping".
Hiding regenerate and edit buttons behind hover states.

Mobile users and many desktop users will never discover hover-only controls. Action buttons on AI responses must always be visible — not just on hover. Users who cannot find regenerate assume the AI is permanently wrong.
No visual distinction between AI and human messages.

If both sides of the conversation look identical, users lose track of who said what, especially when reviewing a long conversation. Use distinct background colors, avatar icons, or alignment (left for AI, right for human).
Instant responses with no thinking indicator.

When a response appears in under 100ms, users suspect pre-canned content. A thinking indicator — even for just 300ms before streaming starts — significantly increases trust scores in usability studies.
Not surfacing what data the AI has access to.

Users who don't know the AI can see their account will repeat information it already has — "my name is Jane, my order number is 12345" — when it could have been inferred. This erodes the perception of intelligence even when the model is working correctly.
Forcing users to retype after a bad response.
If there's no edit-message button, a user whose prompt was misunderstood must retype everything from scratch. This is a significant friction point that causes abandonment in early sessions.
Generic "AI" label with no persona.
Unbranded AI interactions feel uncanny. A named assistant ("Aria from Acme") with a defined scope increases trust and sets clearer expectations about what it can do.
When Affordance Patterns Apply (and When They Don't)
Not every affordance pattern applies to every AI interface. The right affordances depend on how much AI capability is exposed and how familiar users are with AI.
Context
Must Have
Optional
Skip
Consumer chatbot (first-time users)
Starter chips, scoped placeholder, thinking indicator, regenerate button
Capability badges, knowledge cutoff label
Slash commands
Developer / power-user tool (LLM playground)
Token counter, model selector, stop-generation button
Starter chips (power users don't need them)
Scoped placeholder (users know what the model can do)
Embedded copilot in a domain app
Capability badges (showing what data is visible), boundary indicators
Quick-refine chips (shorter, more formal)
Thumbs up/down (too generic for domain context)
AI feature inside an existing app (e.g., smart compose)
Accept/reject controls, undo, subtle streaming cursor
Confidence indicator
Full regenerate flow (too disruptive for embedded AI)
Comparison: Affordance Strategies
There are several competing strategies for surfacing AI capabilities in a UI. Each has different discovery rates, trust outcomes, and implementation costs:
Strategy
Discovery Rate
Trust Impact
Effort
Best For
Starter chips (clickable example prompts)
High (visible by default)
High (shows real output quality)
Low (static copy)
New users, first session
Onboarding walkthrough (interactive tutorial)
High (explicit)
Medium (no live response shown)
High (design + copy)
Complex AI tools with many modes
Proactive suggestions (AI surfaces itself)
Medium (context-dependent)
High if accurate, low if irrelevant
High (trigger logic required)
Embedded copilots with context access
Slash commands menu
Low (requires discovery of / command)
High for power users
Medium (command list + UI)
Developer tools, productivity apps
Feature tooltip on hover
Low (requires cursor hover)
Low (passive, no demonstration)
Low
Secondary controls for experienced users
Best Practices
Scope your placeholder text to the actual domain.
"Ask me about your subscription, billing, or plan upgrades" outperforms "Ask me anything" in every A/B test on conversion and satisfaction.
Show capability badges contextually, not as a wall of text.
"I can see your recent orders" shown in a sidebar when the user navigates to their account is more effective than a capabilities list on the onboarding screen.
Surface boundary affordances at the moment of frustration, not upfront.

A "here's what I can't help with" disclaimer on load creates anxiety. The same information delivered inline when the AI reaches its limit ("That's outside what I can help with — try the returns portal") feels helpful.
Make regenerate and feedback controls always visible on AI bubbles.
Do not hide them behind hover states. These are primary controls that teach the interaction model.
Treat starter chips as a live demo, not just navigation.

When a user clicks a chip, the AI should respond with a high-quality, representative answer. If the chip-triggered answers are poor, remove the chip — a bad first impression is worse than no affordance at all.
Use a named AI persona with a clear "powered by AI" label.
"Aria — AI assistant" satisfies both the trust need (named, accountable entity) and the transparency requirement (explicitly AI, not a human).
Security and Reliability Considerations
Affordances are not just a UX concern — they interact directly with AI safety and reliability properties:
Misleading capability affordances are a safety risk.

If the UI implies the AI has up-to-date medical or legal knowledge when it doesn't, users may act on hallucinated information with real-world consequences. Boundary affordances (knowledge cutoff dates, scope disclaimers) are not optional for high-stakes domains.
Starter chips can be prompt injection vectors.

If chip content is user-configurable or loaded from a database without sanitisation, malicious content can be injected into prompts through the chip mechanism. Treat chip text like any other user input.
Feedback buttons create training data with consent implications.
Thumbs up/down feedback that is used for RLHF must be disclosed in the privacy policy. In EU/GDPR contexts, users must be informed that their feedback is used to improve the model.
Stop-generation buttons must actually stop generation server-side.
Cancelling only the streaming display while the model continues running wastes compute and may log the full response. Implement true cancellation with server-sent abort signals.
Key Takeaways
Affordances in AI UI communicate what the system can do, how to interact with it, and where its limits lie — without requiring users to discover these properties through trial and error.
The four categories of AI affordances are input (how to ask), capability (what the AI knows or can do), boundary (what it cannot do), and interaction (how to control and refine).
Scoped placeholder text, starter chips, and capability badges are the highest-impact affordances for new users — they set accurate expectations before the first interaction.
Regenerate, copy, and feedback controls must be visually present on every AI response — not hidden behind hover states — because they teach users the correction loop.
A thinking indicator within 100ms of prompt submission is mandatory; users who see nothing for 2+ seconds assume the interface is broken.
Boundary affordances (scope indicators, knowledge cutoffs, confidence disclaimers) are not optional in high-stakes domains — misleading capability signals create safety risks.
The affordance audit checklist is a concrete, repeatable tool: walk through each of the eight items at every design review to ensure the interface teaches its own interaction model.
